title: NATO Summit Week Opens With Erdogan Demands, F110 Engine Sale Under Fire, and Israel-Turkey Relations Hit New Low
subhead: One week before the Ankara summit, Erdogan pressed for inclusion in EU defense structures and lifting of arms trade restrictions, while a bipartisan Congressional coalition moved to block the $700M F110 engine sale and Israel formally recognized the Armenian genocide — deepening an already severe diplomatic rupture.

sections.heading: Erdogan Demands Turkey's Inclusion in European Defense Structures Ahead of Ankara Summit
sections.body:
Speaking to NATO parliamentary delegates from all 32 member states in Istanbul on Monday, President Recep Tayyip Erdogan called for Turkey to be included in all European defense and security initiatives, specifically naming the EU's €150 billion ($176 billion) Security Action for Europe (SAFE) program. Under current SAFE rules, non-EU firms can supply only up to 35% of component costs for funded weapons projects. Erdogan demanded that cap be lifted for Turkey, requiring a bilateral security partnership with the EU approved by all 27 member states — a process Greece has already threatened to block.

Erdogan also urged NATO to remove all barriers to defense industry trade between alliance members, arguing that restrictions undermine the alliance's spirit. 'If we want to overcome the challenges we face, we need to remove obstacles to defense industry trade while ensuring balanced and fair burden-sharing,' he said, per Asharq Al-Awsat. He reiterated Turkey's commitment to NATO's defense spending targets while complaining that Turkey's contributions to European security are sometimes overlooked.

EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas, also addressing the NATO Parliamentary Summit, described Turkey as having 'a very, very prominent role' in European security and regional stability, according to Anadolu Agency — though she stopped short of endorsing the SAFE access Erdogan is seeking.
sections.whyItMatters: Erdogan is using the Ankara summit as leverage to break into EU defense procurement — a market Turkey has been excluded from by both its non-EU status and bilateral Greek opposition. The 35% cap is a real constraint on Turkish defense firms, but the political cost of letting Ankara tap €150B in EU defense funds would be significant for several member states.

sections.heading: Bipartisan Push in Congress to Block $700M F110 Engine Sale to Turkey
sections.body:
The Trump administration formally notified Congress on June 24 of a $700+ million sale of General Electric F110 engines to power Turkey's fifth-generation KAAN fighter jet — triggering a 15-day review window. The move was widely interpreted as a pre-summit gesture to Erdogan, with Trump saying he would give Erdogan something that would make him 'very happy' during the Ankara meeting.

But a bipartisan coalition in Congress is mobilizing to block it. Rep. Dina Titus (D-NV) announced she will introduce a Joint Resolution of Disapproval and is circulating a letter to House leadership urging resistance to any Turkish return to the F-35 program. Rep. Gregory Meeks (D-NY), Ranking Member of the House Foreign Affairs Committee, said the administration was bypassing Congressional review 'in yet another deeply troubling example of open contempt for Congress's oversight authority.' Republican opposition came from Reps. Nicole Malliotakis (R-NY), Mike Lawler (R-NY), and Gus Bilirakis (R-FL), who cited Turkey's continued possession of the Russian S-400 system and its destabilizing regional conduct.

Legally, the sale is an ITAR/EAR export license subject to Congressional review. A 'hold' has no legal force — the White House can bypass it. Blocking the sale outright would require a joint resolution of disapproval passed by both chambers and overriding a likely presidential veto, a two-thirds majority — which is unlikely. But the political signal is clear: even as the administration rewards Ankara, Congress remains opposed.

The Armenian National Committee of America (ANCA) and Hellenic American organizations are jointly lobbying against the sale, calling it arming 'an enemy to attack our friends' — per ANCA Executive Director Aram Hamparian, reported by Asbarez.
sections.whyItMatters: The F110 sale is the first major test of whether the Trump administration can normalize defense trade with Turkey despite the S-400/CAATSA sanctions regime. Even if the sale proceeds, Congressional resistance signals that full F-35 reintegration remains off the table — keeping Turkey's defense relationship with Washington in a half-open, half-closed limbo.

sections.heading: Israel Recognizes Armenian Genocide, Deepening Rupture With Turkey
sections.body:
Israel's cabinet unanimously recognized the Armenian genocide on Sunday, in a move Foreign Minister Gideon Sa'ar described as recognition of 'the genocide committed against the Armenian people during the final years of the Ottoman Empire.' Sa'ar explicitly linked the timing to Turkey's conduct, saying Turkey's 'open hostility, inflammatory rhetoric and hostile actions' toward Israel do not grant it 'immunity from historical truth,' per Alhurra.

The decision drew an immediate reaction from Turkey, where officials have long denied that the 1915 events constituted genocide. Azerbaijan also condemned Israel's move, calling it a 'distortion of historical facts' and urging Jerusalem to reconsider, per The Hindu — a notable alignment given Baku's close energy and security ties with Israel.

Former Israeli ambassador to Turkey Alon Liel told Alhurra that Turkey 'has effectively become an enemy state to Israel.' The recognition follows a near-miss naval incident reported by Israel's Channel 12, in which four Turkish destroyers approached an Israeli missile ship during a joint exercise with Greece and Cyprus near Cyprus. Israeli security officials now reportedly regard Turkey as a potential military adversary, not merely a political rival.

The recognition also comes amid Turkey's Sea Wolf 2026 naval exercises, held earlier in June across the Black Sea, Aegean, and Eastern Mediterranean, involving 125 vessels, 60 aircraft, and 18,000 personnel, with tests of domestically developed systems including the AKYA torpedo, ATMACA anti-ship missile, and HISAR-D naval air defense system, per Alhurra.
sections.whyItMatters: The genocide recognition is a diplomatic weapon — Israel is using a contested historical narrative as leverage in an active confrontation with Turkey. Combined with the naval near-miss and the Sea Wolf exercises, the Turkey-Israel relationship has shifted from rhetorical hostility to a credible risk of military escalation in the Eastern Mediterranean.

sections.heading: Turkey-Iran Gas Contract Expires in July — Payment Rail, Not Supply, Is the Real Test
sections.body:
Turkey's 25-year natural gas pipeline contract with Iran expires in July, and the question is no longer whether the gas will flow — it is whether Turkish banks can pay for it. According to a detailed Forbes analysis by Güney Yıldız, the physical supply question has largely been answered: Iran supplied ~7.8 bcm in 2025 (~13% of Turkey's imports), down from once-irreplaceable levels. Long-term LNG deals with ExxonMobil and Shell (signed 2024), plus rising Black Sea Sakarya field output, make Iranian gas nationally optional.

But nationally optional is not locally optional. Eastern Anatolia's infrastructure was built for east-to-west gas flows, and replacing those volumes with western LNG during a cold snap requires reverse-flow capacity the system does not yet have. The largest new LNG cargoes only start arriving in 2027.

The binding constraint is the payment rail. Three developments converge: the September 2025 JCPOA snapback restoring UN sanctions on Iran; the June 2026 Halkbank deferred prosecution agreement that bars the bank from transactions benefiting Iran while installing an independent monitor; and a US OFAC general license covering Iranian oil and petrochemicals through August 21 that pointedly excludes natural gas. No one has formally banned Turkish banks from settling gas payments to Tehran. The combination of a monitored institution, restored UN sanctions, and a gas-shaped hole in the OFAC license is enough to make compliance officers hesitate.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the first live test of whether the 2026 sanctions architecture can close the 'gray zones' that previously let energy links survive between sanctioned states and their customers. If payment channels freeze, the pipeline stops regardless of Ankara's preferences — and eastern Anatolia enters winter with less flexibility.

sections.heading: Kurdish Peace Process: Rallies in Diyarbakir Call for Öcalan's Release
sections.body:
Several thousand people rallied Sunday in Diyarbakir, the Kurdish-majority city in southeast Turkey, calling for the release of jailed PKK leader Abdullah Öcalan, according to Asharq Al-Awsat. The rallies come amid an ongoing — if fragile — peace process between Ankara and the PKK, with Öcalan having issued a call from prison in early 2025 for the group to disarm.

The Diyarbakir rallies signal that Kurdish civil society is pressing for tangible progress on Öcalan's release as a benchmark for the process's credibility. Erdogan, in his NATO parliamentary address on Monday, said Ankara expected 'more from allies to support its efforts to end the PKK's more than 40-year insurgency,' per Cyprus Mail — an unusual framing that ties the Kurdish peace process to NATO burden-sharing demands.
sections.whyItMatters: The Kurdish peace process is still alive, but the Diyarbakir rallies show that patience on the ground is finite. Erdogan's attempt to link the PKK issue to NATO solidarity is a diplomatic gambit — it gives Ankara another ask at the summit, but whether NATO allies see PKK disarmament as their burden to share is an open question.

sections.heading: Iraq Sends Foreign Minister to Damascus in First Post-Assad Visit
sections.body:
Iraqi Foreign Minister Fuad Hussein arrived in Damascus on Monday for the first high-level Iraqi visit since the fall of Assad in December 2024, meeting with Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa, per Kurdistan24 and Iraq News. The talks covered border security, counterterrorism cooperation against ISIS, trade, and the possible revival of the Kirkuk–Baniyas oil pipeline — dormant for over two decades.

The visit follows the June 2025 reopening of the al-Qaim border crossing and al-Shaibani's March 2025 trip to Baghdad. Both governments stressed shared security concerns along their 600-kilometer border, where ISIS remnants still operate. Hussein also extended an invitation for al-Sharaa to visit Baghdad.

For Turkey, the Iraq-Syria rapprochement matters because it creates a new axis of coordination along Turkey's southern flank — one that could either support or complicate Ankara's own dealings with the new Syrian government, depending on how Baghdad and Damascus align their positions on Kurdish groups and border management.
sections.whyItMatters: A functional Iraq-Syria border and intelligence relationship directly affects Turkey's security calculus. If Baghdad and Damascus coordinate on counter-ISIS and border control, it could reduce the pretext for Turkish cross-border operations. If they don't, Turkey retains its rationale for military presence in northern Syria.

bottomLine: Seven days before Turkey hosts the NATO summit, Erdogan is making his ask list public: inclusion in EU defense procurement, lifting of arms trade restrictions, and more allied support against the PKK. The Trump administration's pre-summit F110 engine gift is already under bipartisan fire in Congress, and Israel's recognition of the Armenian genocide has turned an already hostile relationship into one with credible military escalation risk. Meanwhile, the expiration of the Iran gas contract is less a supply story than a sanctions-compliance test — and Iraqi diplomacy with the new Syrian government is reshaping the security picture on Turkey's southern border. The Ankara summit will be the stage where all these threads converge.
sources: Reuters, Asharq Al-Awsat, Cyprus Mail, Forbes (Güney Yıldız), Anadolu Agency, Daily Sabah, Alhurra, Asbarez, Protothema, Kurdistan24, Iraq News, The Hindu, Seeking Alpha, Novinite
